title: Multiscale Phase Separation in Chemophoretic Active Matter
authors: Manisha Jhajhria, Subir K. Das, Snigdha Thakur
abstract: Nonreciprocal interactions in active matter provide interesting structure and dynamics. Here we investigate chemophoretic systems in which nonreciprocity arises from the asymmetric coupling between agents: first species produces certain chemicals and the other phoretically responds to it. This leads to phase separation at varying scales. Our study uncovers a re-entrant steady-state phase diagram as the nature of the coupling changes from chemoattractive to chemorepulsive character. Chemoattraction provides sustained domain growth, leading to macrophase separation via cluster coalescence. Aggregation in the chemorepulsive case, on the other hand, leads to a steady-state situation that displays phase separation only at a microscale, owing to strong caging effect and frequent fragmentation. The overall far-from-steady-state dynamics is quantified via calculations of growth exponents, cluster transition matrices, and mean-squared displacements.
summary: This work analyzes phase separation in active matter driven by chemical gradients, contrasting chemoattractive and chemorepulsive coupling. The key finding is that attraction leads to continuous, macroscopic domain growth, whereas repulsion causes growth to arrest at a microscale due to dynamical trapping. The study uses advanced diagnostics like growth exponents and transition matrices to distinguish these fundamentally different non-equilibrium pathways.
main_problem: The study investigates how nonreciprocal interactions in chemophoretic active matter lead to multiscale phase separation, comparing the kinetics in chemoattractive versus chemorepulsive coupling regimes.
main_result: Chemoattraction drives sustained, macrophase separation via cluster coalescence, while chemorepulsion leads to arrested, microscale phase separation due to strong caging and fragmentation.
method: The research employs simulations and scaling analysis using the two-point equal-time correlation function, cluster transition matrices, and Mean-Squared Displacement (MSD) to characterize growth dynamics.
model_or_system: The system models binary mixtures of chemically active source (S) and phoretic (P) particles whose motion is governed by Langevin dynamics and asymmetric chemophoretic forces. The interaction strength is controlled by the parameter k, determining attraction or repulsion.
key_observables: Growth exponents ($\alpha$), fractal dimension ($d_f$), cluster transition matrices, and Mean-Squared Displacements (MSD).
important_parameters: Phoretic strength parameter $k$ (sign determines attraction/repulsion), particle density ($\phi$), and temperature ($k_B T$).
main_assumptions: The analysis involves mapping continuum systems to a 2D square lattice and applying coarse-graining techniques to analyze domain morphology.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate simulation snapshots comparing aggregation in both regimes, phase coexistence curves, and the evolution of cluster size and MSD, showing distinct scaling behaviors.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares the phase-ordering kinetics by analyzing the structural evolution (correlation functions), the cluster size dynamics (transition matrices and growth/fragmentation bias), and the particle transport (MSD) across the chemoattractive ($k>0$) and chemorepulsive ($k<0$) regimes.
why_interesting: The study provides a detailed, non-equilibrium characterization of phase separation driven by active, non-reciprocal forces, offering insights into pattern formation far from equilibrium that can inform models of active matter dynamics relevant to AMO systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Steady-state coexistence curves for the binary sus- pensions in the phoretic strength (k) vs local packing fraction (ϕ) plane. For k < 0, the system exhibits a phase separation due to chemorepulsion (red), and for k > 0, the system mod- els chemoattraction (blue).

FIG. 1. Instantaneous late time (t = 2000) simulation snapshots showing aggregation in binary mixtures of source (red) and phoretic (blue) particles. The top panels display chemoattractive cases with clusters containing both S and P particles. The bottom panels show cases for chemorepulsion, where the clusters consist solely of P particles. The diffusio- phoretic strength in both cases has been kept at |k| = 1, 2, 50. Only (L/4)×(L/4) parts of the original system (L = 150) are shown for clarity.

FIG. 3. Two-point equal time correlation function, C(r, t), is plotted against r. In (a) and (b) we show results for the chemoattractive case with k = 10 and k = 3, respectively. The insets of these figures show the scaling plots of C(r, t), where the distance is divided by the time-dependent domain lengths ℓ(t). In (c) and (d), we show C(r, t) for the chemore- pulsive case with k = −10 and k = −50, respectively. Due to the fractal nature of clusters in this system, the scaling form of C(r, t) gets modified via the introduction of df. The dashed horizontal lines represent the ordinate value “0”.

FIG. 4. Evolutions of domain lengths ℓ(t) for different k val- ues, for the cases of (a) chemoattraction and (b) chemorepul- sion. These results are obtained from the scaling of C(r, t). The insets of both (a) and (b) show the instantaneous growth exponent, αi, as a function of 1/ℓ(t). The arrow-headed lines point towards the ℓ→∞limit. (c-d) Evolutions of maximum cluster size (Nmax), as a function of time, for chemoattractive and chemorepulsive cases, respectively.

FIG. 5. Transition probability matrix P(N1|N0, τs), analyzed for time interval τs = 10τ, for (a) chemoattractive (k = 3) and (b) chemorepulsive (k = −50) cases. The color-bar represents P(N1|N0, τs) on log scale. Insets of (a) and (b) show the number of particles in clusters of different sizes tracked over time t. (c) Conditional growth-fragmentation bias B(N0), for k = 3, 50 and k = −50, are shown against N0. Here B > 0 indicates growth dominated dynamics and B < 0 means fragmentation-dominated dynamics.
